summary: McKenzie shares his strategy for passing the Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT), arguing that mastering kanji through daily spaced-repetition practice is the most efficient path to passing. He breaks down the test's scoring structure to show that the listening section is easy enough to coast on, meaning candidates only need slightly better than 50% on the remaining sections. He recommends a specific flashcard software called ReadWrite Kanji for daily drilling, emphasizing that true mastery means instant recall without hesitation. The post is explicitly a proof-of-concept for his advice on writing customer-focused blog content that naturally incorporates product recommendations.
key_points: The JLPT listening section is disproportionately easy, so a strategic test-taker should focus effort on kanji mastery to cover the remaining points needed
True kanji mastery means instant, unhesitating recall — not vague recognition — and the test is specifically designed to punish fuzzy knowledge with near-miss answer choices
Spaced-repetition software beats physical flashcards by eliminating the overhead of manually sorting cards into mastery tiers
The post itself is a deliberate exercise in writing content marketing that provides genuine value while naturally recommending a product
money_quotes: Taking level 4 is a waste of your time and money, because nobody cares that you have a piece of paper that says you can order a beer.
The listening comprehension section is a joke.
People hate rote memorization, and they hate getting up every day and doing 20 minutes of kanji practice. But if you do it, and if you start early enough, you'll steamroll the JLPT.
No hesitation, no fudging, no peaking at the other side of your flashcard and saying 'Oh, yeah, I knew that': you look at that problem for 3 seconds and say BAM.
With only kana you're not even good enough to be called functionally illiterate.
Whoops, sorry, that was my freshman year Japanese textbooks. No, its actually $16.
key_insight: Strategic test preparation means identifying the easiest scoring sections to coast on and concentrating effort on the highest-leverage skill — in this case, kanji mastery through daily spaced-repetition drilling.
